original_abstract: Time is the most precious resource we have. It is irreversible and nonrenewable. It makes the difference, more than ever, between the best and worst scenarios of climate change, energy competition, economic development, poverty, and security. Despite this, an incredible amount of time is wasted, especially the time of others and of nature. These latter resources are needed to prevent violence, build sustainable security, and ensure the well-being of all. Therefore, it is high time to radically change the way we deal with time and to develop a more adaptive temporament. This article defines time, surveys temporal deficiencies, and presents the parameters of a more responsible way of dealing with time in conflict transformation.
humanized_abstract: Time is a limited resource that is irreversible and non-renewable. It is more than ever the factor which determines whether the consequences of climate change, energy conflicts, economic development, poverty and insecurity will be favourable or catastrophic. At the same time, an enormous amount of time is lost on a daily basis, time that belongs to others and to nature. These other resources are essential for combating violence and building sustainable security and for the well-being of all. It is therefore high time for us to rethink our relation to time and to develop a temporament that is capable of adapting to the urgent challenges of our time. This article begins by providing a definition of time and an overview of the gaps in relation to time that need to be addressed in order to ensure an adequate temporament for conflict transformation and then presents the parameters of a more time-efficient way of working in the context of peacebuilding, taking into account recent empirical research.
